runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity of labor supply for prime-age U.S. workers already employed: the percent change in annual hours from a 1% change in the net-of-tax wage, holding marginal utility of wealth constant (excluding participation responses).
citations: Chetty et al. (micro evidence on labor supply elasticities and Frisch/income-substitution concepts)
Keane & Rogerson / MaCurdy tradition of estimating labor-supply Frisch-type elasticities from panel earnings and hours
Standard life-cycle/macro calibration ranges using Frisch elasticity around 0.5–1.0 for employed workers
runs.reasoningSummary: Prime-age intensive-margin hours responses to net-of-tax wage changes are typically modest: many empirical and calibration strands imply elasticities well below 2, with a central tendency around ~0.6–0.9; I allow a lower tail near ~0.2 and an upper tail around ~1.4 for heterogeneity and identification uncertainty.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours Frisch elasticity: the percent change in annual hours worked from a 1% change in the net-of-tax wage, holding marginal utility of wealth constant and excluding participation responses, for prime-age U.S. workers who are already employed.

runs.reasoningSummary: My prior is that intensive-margin labor supply responses to wage changes are positive but modest for employed prime-age workers; values around 0.5–1.0 are plausible, with heavier tails allowing somewhat larger elasticities, but low outcomes still possible due to strong preferences for stable working hours and slow substitution/adjustment.

runs.reasoningSummary: For prime-age workers already employed, I currently expect a modest but nontrivial elasticity: hours respond to net-of-tax wages, but mostly within ranges limited by preferences, job characteristics, and frictions. I place the median around 0.4, with a lower tail near 0.1 and an upper tail around 1.1 reflecting less common specifications/segments with stronger labor supply responsiveness.
